{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].

Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.

Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].

Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.

However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.

revision.text:
{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== See Also == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== See Also == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== See Also == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== See Also == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== See Also == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== See Also == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

[[sn:Tsuchimikado Harenaga]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== See Also == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox politician | name = Tsuchimikado Harenaga | native_name = 土御門 晴栄 | image = Harenaga Tsuchimikado.jpg | caption = Tsuchimikado Harenaga in 1913 | birth_date = {{Birth date|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | birth_place = [[Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{Death date and age|1915|10|16|1859|6|30|df=yes}} | death_place = | nationality = Japanese | occupation = [[Onmyōji]], Politician | spouse = | children = [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]] | office = Member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] | term_start = June 21, 1900 | term_end = October 16, 1915 | party = Independent | rank = [[Viscount]] | awards = [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]] }}
'''Tsuchimikado Harenaga''' (土御門 晴栄, 1859–1915) was a Japanese [[onmyōji]] (master of yin-yang), scholar, and politician. He held the hereditary title of [[viscount]] (子爵) and served as a member of the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] during the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[Taishō era|Taishō]] periods.
== Overview == Harenaga was the head of the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|ja|土御門家}}—a family historically associated with [[onmyōdō]] practices, [[astronomy]], and [[calendar-making]] (暦道). Despite being a direct descendant of the famed [[Abe no Seimei]], he faced numerous challenges after inheriting the clan during the societal transformations of the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The abolition of the [[Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) marked the loss of the clan’s traditional privileges, including its authority over calendar-making.
Harenaga later became involved in politics, serving three terms in the House of Peers. His contributions as a public official earned him the posthumous honor of [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]].
== Early Life == Harenaga was born in 1859 as the second son of {{ill|Nishigori Hisataka|ja|錦織久隆}}, a noble of the {{ill|Nishigori clan|ja|錦織家}}. Through maternal lines, he was connected to prominent ancestors in the Tsuchimikado and Nishigori families, who were noted for their service in onmyōdō and court positions.
In 1869, he was adopted by {{ill|Tsuchimikado Haruo|ja|土御門晴雄}} and became the head of the Tsuchimikado family following Haruo’s sudden death. Around this time, he was tasked by the [[Meiji government]] to oversee the remnants of onmyōdō practices. However, as the government centralized the calendar-making authority under a new [[Astronomical Bureau]], the Kyoto-based Tsuchimikado family lost its influence.
== Career == === Onmyōji and Astronomical Duties === In the early Meiji period, Harenaga was appointed as an official for astronomical and calendrical tasks within the newly formed {{ill|Astronomy Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}} under the [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. However, after the Bureau's relocation to Tokyo, the Tsuchimikado family became a peripheral institution. Subsequent reorganizations eventually stripped the family of its remaining responsibilities in this field.
=== Political Career === In 1884, Harenaga was elevated to the rank of viscount under the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}}, becoming part of the newly established [[kazoku]] aristocracy. He entered the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]] in 1900, representing the viscounts. Serving three terms, he advocated for various reforms and remained active in the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Parliament]] until his death in 1915.
== Personal Life == Harenaga married the daughter of [[Reizei Tameyoshi]], further cementing his family’s noble ties. He adopted his son-in-law, [[Tsuchimikado Haruyuki]], to succeed him as the head of the Tsuchimikado family and as a viscount.
== Honors == * 1884: Elevated to [[Viscount]] (子爵) * 1894: Received the rank of [[Fourth Rank|Junior Fourth Rank]] * 1915: Posthumously awarded [[Third Rank|Senior Third Rank]]
== Legacy == Harenaga's efforts to preserve the heritage of the Tsuchimikado clan amidst political upheavals remain notable. Although the family lost its traditional authority in onmyōdō, its legacy continues through descendants and scholarly work.
== See Also == * {{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|ja|貴族院}}
